 {"id":"bd-l9k8e.1","title":"C1: Design the CellVisibilityLog data structure and cell-level MVCC architecture","description":"## What

Design the core data structure that replaces full-page version chains for ordinary row operations. This is the single most important design decision in the project right now.

## Background: Why Page-Level Is Wrong

Currently (crates/fsqlite-mvcc/src/invariants.rs), PageVersion stores:
  - pgno: PageNumber, commit_seq: CommitSeq, created_by: TxnToken, data: PageData (FULL 4KB page clone), prev_idx: Option<VersionIdx>

A version chain for page 47 might look like: [v5: full 4KB] -> [v4: full 4KB] -> [v3: full 4KB]

If txn A inserts row 1000 and txn B inserts row 1001, and both land on page 47:
  - Current: PAGE-LEVEL CONFLICT. One wins, one retries.
  - Desired: NO CONFLICT. Both rows are on the same page but different cells.

## Design Requirements

1. CellVisibilityLog: In-memory structure keyed by (PageNumber, CellIndex) or (PageNumber, RowId) that records what op happened, which txn, commit_seq, and enough info to reconstruct cell content without whole-page cloning.

2. Snapshot visibility at cell granularity: Given snapshot S and page P, answer "which version of cell C is visible to S?" in O(1) or O(log n), NOT O(chain_length).

3. On-disk pages remain SQLite-compatible. CellVisibilityLog is purely in-memory. At persistence boundaries (checkpoint, WAL write), materialize logical deltas into real page images.

4. Two operation classes: LOGICAL (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE without structural change) -> CellVisibilityLog only; STRUCTURAL (splits, merges, freelist, rebalance) -> full-page versioning.

## Design Options to Evaluate

Option A: Slot-indexed delta log. Key: (PageNumber, SlotIndex). Pro: direct B-tree cell array mapping. Con: slot indices shift on insert/delete.

Option B: RowId-indexed delta log. Key: (PageNumber, RowId) for tables, (PageNumber, IndexKey) for indexes. Pro: stable keys. Con: more complex.

Option C: Hybrid — slot-indexed with RowId for conflict resolution. Use slots for fast access, record RowId so structural changes can re-derive positions.

## Postgres Reference (already at /tmp/postgres-frankensqlite-study-20260319)
- htup_details.h:122 — tuple header with xmin/xmax
- snapshot.h:138 — compact Snapshot struct
- heapam.c:522 — batched page visibility
- pruneheap.c:199 — opportunistic pruning

Key insight: Postgres xmin/xmax lives WITH the tuple. We keep SQLite-compatible on-disk format but put equivalent metadata in a memory sidecar.

## Deliverables
1. Written design doc with Rust type signatures, memory layout, overhead per cell, visibility resolution, structural interaction, persistence/checkpoint materialization, GC strategy.
2. Stub types in crates/fsqlite-mvcc/src/ (shape only, no implementation).","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":480,"created_at":"2026-03-19T20:10:17.782736970Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-19T20:10:17.782736970Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","design","mvcc","track-c"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-l9k8e.1","depends_on_id":"bd-l9k8e","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-19T20:10:17.782736970Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-l9k8e.2","title":"C2: Define the structural vs logical operation boundary","description":"## What

Enumerate every B-tree mutation in the codebase and classify it as LOGICAL (cell-level MVCC) or STRUCTURAL (page-level MVCC). This classification is load-bearing: it determines which operations use the cheap new path and which stay on the existing expensive path.

## Why This Matters

The whole point of the MVCC redesign is that MOST writes are logical (they change cell contents but don't change page structure). Only a minority trigger structural changes. If we get this boundary wrong, either:
- Too many ops stay on the page path (no improvement), or
- Structural ops accidentally use the cell path (corruption)

## Classification Criteria

LOGICAL (cell-level, cheap path):
- INSERT into a leaf page that has room (no split needed)
- UPDATE that doesn't change cell size enough to require overflow/restructure
- DELETE from a leaf page that doesn't trigger merge
- Index INSERT/DELETE that fits in existing page

STRUCTURAL (page-level, existing path):
- Page split (leaf or interior)
- Page merge / rebalance
- Overflow chain creation or modification
- Freelist allocation / deallocation
- Root page promotion (tree height increase)
- Page type changes
- Schema changes (sqlite_schema modifications)

EDGE CASES to resolve:
- INSERT that fits in the page but changes the cell pointer array offset — is this structural? (No: cell pointer array is part of page layout, but adding a cell to an existing page is the common case. The cell pointer array shift is a page-local bookkeeping detail that we can handle in materialization.)
- UPDATE that changes cell size — at what threshold does this become structural? (When overflow is needed or when the page exceeds usable space.)
- DELETE that leaves the page underfull — do we eagerly merge or lazily? (Lazily: mark as underfull, merge on next structural operation or checkpoint.)

## Where to Look

Key B-tree mutation code lives in:
- crates/fsqlite-btree/src/lib.rs — insert, delete, update cell operations
- crates/fsqlite-btree/src/cursor.rs — cursor-based mutations
- crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/engine.rs — VDBE opcodes that trigger mutations (Insert, Delete, IdxInsert, IdxDelete, NewRowid)
- crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs — high-level execute paths that route to B-tree ops

## Deliverables

1. A classification table (in code comments or docs/) listing every B-tree mutation function and its classification.
2. A predicate function signature: fn is_structural_mutation(op: &BtreeOp, page: &Page) -> bool
3. Clear documentation of edge cases and the rationale for each decision.

## Depends on: C1 (need the target architecture to classify against)","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":240,"created_at":"2026-03-19T20:10:40.817417708Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-19T20:10:40.817417708Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","mvcc","track-c"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-l9k8e.2","depends_on_id":"bd-l9k8e","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-19T20:10:40.817417708Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-l9k8e.3","title":"C3: Implement CellVisibilityLog and cell-level snapshot resolution","description":"## What

Implement the CellVisibilityLog data structure designed in C1. This is the core new component that makes cell-level MVCC work.

## Context

This lives in crates/fsqlite-mvcc/. It sits alongside the existing VersionStore (invariants.rs) and lifecycle management (lifecycle.rs). It does NOT replace VersionStore — it handles the common case (logical row ops) while VersionStore continues to handle the rare case (structural page ops).

## Key Components to Implement

### 1. CellDelta struct
Records a single logical operation on a cell:
- txn_id: TxnId (who did this)
- commit_seq: CommitSeq (when committed; UNCOMMITTED sentinel if still active)
- op: CellOp (Insert, Update, Delete)
- cell_data: Option<Vec<u8>> (the cell content for Insert/Update; None for Delete)
- prev_commit_seq: CommitSeq (the version this delta supersedes, for GC)

### 2. CellVisibilityLog struct
The main index:
- Map from (PageNumber, CellKey) -> SmallVec<[CellDelta; 2]>
  where CellKey is RowId for table pages, (encoded_key_prefix, RowId) for index pages
- The SmallVec is ordered by commit_seq descending (newest first)
- Sharded by PageNumber for concurrency (like existing PageLockTable: 64 shards, cache-line padded)

### 3. Snapshot visibility resolution
fn resolve_cell(log: &CellVisibilityLog, pgno: PageNumber, key: CellKey, snapshot: &Snapshot) -> CellState
- CellState: Visible(cell_data), Deleted, NotTracked (fall through to page-level)
- Walk the SmallVec until commit_seq <= snapshot.high; return that delta
- If no delta matches, this cell has no logged changes -> read from base page

### 4. Transaction write set (cell-level)
When a txn writes a cell, record it in the log with commit_seq = UNCOMMITTED.
At COMMIT: atomically update all UNCOMMITTED entries for this txn to the assigned CommitSeq.
At ROLLBACK: remove all UNCOMMITTED entries for this txn.

### 5. Conflict detection (cell-level)
Two txns conflict if they touch the same (PageNumber, CellKey) and their active intervals overlap.
This REPLACES page-level conflict detection for logical operations.
Page-level conflicts only trigger for structural operations.

### 6. GC
A CellDelta is reclaimable when:
- A newer committed delta for the same cell exists, AND
- Both deltas are below the GC horizon (min of all active snapshots)
This mirrors the existing page-level GC logic but at cell granularity.

## Integration Points
- VersionStore (invariants.rs): CellVisibilityLog is a sibling, not a replacement. The read path tries CellVisibilityLog first; if NotTracked, falls through to VersionStore.
- lifecycle.rs: commit/rollback paths need to handle both cell-level and page-level entries.
- begin_concurrent.rs: conflict detection needs to check cell-level conflicts before page-level.

## Testing Strategy
- Unit tests: insert/update/delete + visibility resolution at various snapshot points
- Conflict tests: two txns writing same cell -> conflict; different cells same page -> no conflict
- GC tests: entries cleaned after horizon advances
- Mixed tests: some logical ops + a structural op on same page -> correct fallback

## Depends on: C1 (design), C2 (boundary classification)","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":960,"created_at":"2026-03-19T20:11:06.293519454Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-19T20:11:06.293519454Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["implementation","mvcc","track-c"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-l9k8e.3","depends_on_id":"bd-l9k8e","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-19T20:11:06.293519454Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-l9k8e.4","title":"C4: Wire cell-level deltas into the concurrent writer commit path","description":"## What

Connect the CellVisibilityLog (C3) to the actual transaction commit path so that logical row operations go through the cheap cell-level path instead of creating full-page version copies.

## Context

The current commit path in crates/fsqlite-mvcc/src/lifecycle.rs does:
1. Collect dirty pages from the transaction write set
2. For each dirty page, publish a full PageVersion into the VersionStore
3. Validate SSI constraints at page granularity
4. Append WAL frames for the full page images
5. Release page locks

The new commit path for LOGICAL operations:
1. Collect cell-level deltas from the transaction
2. Publish CellDelta entries (cheap: just a few bytes per cell, not 4KB per page)
3. Validate SSI constraints at CELL granularity (much cheaper)
4. WAL write is deferred — cell deltas are memory-only until checkpoint
5. Release cell-level locks (NOT page locks for logical ops)

## Key Changes

### In lifecycle.rs: publish_write_set()
Currently (line ~1425): iterates dirty pages, creates PageVersion for each.
Change: classify each dirty page's changes (using C2 boundary):
  - If ALL changes on a page are logical -> publish to CellVisibilityLog
  - If ANY change on a page is structural -> publish full PageVersion (existing path)
  - This is the critical routing decision.

### In begin_concurrent.rs: commit_prep()
Currently (line ~1948): FCW validation at page granularity, SSI witness hydration.
Change: for pages that went through the cell-level path, FCW checks cell-level base drift instead of page-level base drift. Two txns touching different cells on the same page NO LONGER CONFLICT.

### In lifecycle.rs: WAL write
Cell-level deltas don't immediately produce WAL frames. Instead:
  - At checkpoint time, or when a page accumulates enough deltas, materialize the full page image from (base_page + applied_deltas) and write the WAL frame.
  - This amortizes the page-materialization cost across many logical writes.
  - The materialization function: fn materialize_page(base: &PageData, deltas: &[CellDelta]) -> PageData

### In connection.rs: BEGIN CONCURRENT
The Connection already auto-promotes BEGIN to BEGIN CONCURRENT. No change needed here. But the CONCURRENT transaction's internal write tracking needs to distinguish cell-level from page-level writes.

## Correctness Requirements
- If power fails before checkpoint, uncommitted cell deltas are lost (they are in-memory only). This is FINE because:
  - The WAL already handles crash recovery for committed data
  - Cell deltas for committed txns that haven't been checkpointed yet need to be WAL-recoverable
  - Solution: at commit time for cell-level deltas, write a lightweight "cell-delta WAL record" that can reconstruct the delta on recovery. This is much smaller than a full page image.

## Testing
- Integration test: two concurrent txns inserting to different rows on same page -> both commit
- Integration test: two concurrent txns updating same row -> one retries (cell-level conflict)
- Crash recovery test: cell-delta WAL records survive crash and replay correctly
- Perf test: measure commit latency with cell-level vs page-level path

## Depends on: C3 (CellVisibilityLog implementation)","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":720,"created_at":"2026-03-19T20:11:44.975000363Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-19T20:11:44.975000363Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["implementation","mvcc","track-c"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-l9k8e.4","depends_on_id":"bd-l9k8e","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-19T20:11:44.975000363Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-l9k8e.5","title":"C5: Materialize full pages only at structural/persistence boundaries","description":"## What

Implement the page materialization layer that converts accumulated cell-level deltas into full page images when (and only when) required: structural B-tree changes, WAL checkpointing, and persistence boundaries.

## Why

Cell-level MVCC avoids creating full page copies on every row write. But eventually we need real pages:
- When the B-tree needs to split/merge a page (structural operation)
- When WAL checkpoint writes pages back to the main database file
- When a reader needs a consistent page image for a complex scan

The materialization function applies deltas to a base page to produce the current page image. This is the bridge between the cheap cell-level world and the page-level world.

## Key Implementation

### materialize_page()
fn materialize_page(base: &PageData, deltas: &[CellDelta], snapshot: &Snapshot) -> PageData

This function:
1. Starts with the base page (the last full page version from VersionStore or disk)
2. Applies each CellDelta visible to the snapshot in commit_seq order:
   - CellOp::Insert -> add the cell to the page's cell array at the correct position
   - CellOp::Update -> replace the cell at the matching RowId
   - CellOp::Delete -> remove the cell from the page
3. Returns a complete, valid B-tree page

This requires understanding B-tree page layout (from crates/fsqlite-btree/):
- Page header (8 or 12 bytes)
- Cell pointer array
- Free space
- Cell content area

### Trigger points

1. **Structural operation detected** (page split, merge):
   - Before the split/merge, materialize the page with all committed deltas
   - Feed the materialized page to the existing B-tree split/merge code
   - After the structural op, clear the CellVisibilityLog entries for this page
   - The split/merge result goes through the page-level VersionStore as before

2. **WAL checkpoint**:
   - For each page with outstanding cell deltas:
     - Materialize the page at the checkpoint snapshot
     - Write the materialized page to the main database file
     - Clear deltas that are now below the GC horizon

3. **Eager materialization threshold**:
   - If a page accumulates > N deltas (tunable, maybe 32 or 64), materialize eagerly
   - This bounds the cost of delta application on reads and prevents unbounded delta growth
   - The materialized page becomes the new base in VersionStore; deltas are cleared

### Read path integration

When a reader needs page P at snapshot S:
1. Check CellVisibilityLog for page P
2. If no deltas: fall through to VersionStore (existing path)
3. If deltas exist:
   a. Get base page from VersionStore
   b. Apply visible deltas to produce the page image
   c. Cache the materialized page for subsequent reads at this snapshot

The caching in step (c) is important: we don't want to re-apply deltas on every read.

## Correctness Invariants
- materialize_page() must produce a valid B-tree page (correct header, cell pointers, free space)
- The cell pointer array must be sorted correctly after delta application
- Overflow chains must be handled (if a cell update changes size enough to need overflow)
- The materialized page must be byte-identical to what would have been produced by applying the operations directly to the B-tree

## Testing
- Unit test: apply insert/update/delete deltas to a known base page, verify result
- Round-trip test: insert N rows via cell deltas, materialize, read back -> identical to direct insert
- Split trigger test: accumulate inserts until page is full, verify materialization triggers before split
- Checkpoint test: write deltas, checkpoint, verify main DB file has correct pages

## Depends on: C3 (CellVisibilityLog), C4 (commit path wiring)","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":720,"created_at":"2026-03-19T20:12:11.047806369Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-19T20:12:11.047806369Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["btree","implementation","mvcc","track-c"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-l9k8e.5","depends_on_id":"bd-l9k8e","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-19T20:12:11.047806369Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-lldk","title":"§11.7-11.9 Record Format + WAL Header + WAL Frame Header + Checksum Algorithm","description":"## SUMMARY

Covers record format (header_size varint + serial_types + data), WAL header (32 bytes), WAL frame header (24 bytes), and the WAL checksum algorithm. The record format uses serial types to encode both type and size for each column value, with special encodings for NULL (0 bytes), small integers (0 bytes for 0/1), and variable-length text/blob. The WAL header contains magic number (endianness-dependent), format version 3007000, page size, checkpoint sequence, salt pair, and header checksum. Frame headers carry page number, commit db-size, salt copies, and cumulative checksum.

## KEY DATA STRUCTURES / ALGORITHMS

- **Record format**: `[header_size: varint] [serial_types: varint...] [data: bytes...]`. header_size varint includes itself in the count.
- **Serial type table**:
  - 0: NULL (0 content bytes)
  - 1: 8-bit signed int (1 byte)
  - 2: 16-bit BE signed int (2 bytes)
  - 3: 24-bit BE signed int (3 bytes)
  - 4: 32-bit BE signed int (4 bytes)
  - 5: 48-bit BE signed int (6 bytes)
  - 6: 64-bit BE signed int (8 bytes)
  - 7: IEEE 754 64-bit float BE (8 bytes)
  - 8: Integer constant 0 (0 bytes)
  - 9: Integer constant 1 (0 bytes)
  - 10, 11: Reserved (internal use)
  - N >= 12, even: BLOB of (N-12)/2 bytes
  - N >= 13, odd: TEXT of (N-13)/2 bytes
- **Worked example**: Row (42, "hello", 3.14, NULL, X'CAFE'): serial types [1, 23, 7, 0, 16], header [06, 01, 17, 07, 00, 10], data [2A, 68656C6C6F, 40091EB851EB851F, (empty), CAFE]. Total 22 bytes.
- **WAL header (32 bytes)**: magic u32 (0x377F0682 LE or 0x377F0683 BE) at offset 0, format version u32 (3007000) at offset 4, page size u32 at offset 8, checkpoint seq u32 at offset 12, salt-1 u32 at offset 16, salt-2 u32 at offset 20, checksum-1 u32 at offset 24, checksum-2 u32 at offset 28.
- **WAL frame header (24 bytes)**: page number u32 at offset 0, db size (for commit frames, else 0) u32 at offset 4, salt-1 u32 at offset 8, salt-2 u32 at offset 12, cumulative checksum-1 u32 at offset 16, cumulative checksum-2 u32 at offset 20.
- **WAL checksum chain**: Header checksum seeds first frame; each frame's checksum covers frame_header[0..8] + page_data, seeded from previous frame's checksum. Cumulative chain. Frame valid iff checksum matches AND salt matches header.

## NORMATIVE INVARIANTS

1. Record header_size varint includes itself in the byte count.
2. Serial types 10 and 11 are reserved for internal use; MUST NOT appear in user data.
3. Integer serial types (1-6) are big-endian signed; the smallest fitting type SHOULD be used.
4. Serial types 8 and 9 encode constants 0 and 1 with zero content bytes (space optimization).
5. BLOB serial type: `N = 2 * byte_count + 12` (even). TEXT serial type: `N = 2 * byte_count + 13` (odd).
6. WAL magic number encodes endianness: 0x377F0682 for little-endian checksum, 0x377F0683 for big-endian.
7. WAL format version MUST be 3007000 (constant for all WAL1 databases).
8. Frame header db-size field is non-zero ONLY for commit frames (indicates database size after commit).
9. Frame salt values MUST match WAL header salt; mismatch invalidates the frame.
10. Checksum chain is cumulative: each frame depends on all previous frames.

## UNIT TEST REQUIREMENTS

1. `test_record_format_null` -- NULL value: serial type 0, 0 content bytes.
2. `test_record_format_int8` -- Value 42: serial type 1, 1 content byte [0x2A].
3. `test_record_format_int16` -- Value 256: serial type 2, 2 content bytes [0x01, 0x00].
4. `test_record_format_int64` -- Large value: serial type 6, 8 content bytes BE.
5. `test_record_format_float` -- 3.14: serial type 7, 8 content bytes IEEE 754 BE.
6. `test_record_format_zero_one_constants` -- Value 0: serial type 8 (0 bytes). Value 1: serial type 9 (0 bytes).
7. `test_record_format_text` -- "hello" (5 bytes): serial type 23 (5*2+13), 5 content bytes.
8. `test_record_format_blob` -- X'CAFE' (2 bytes): serial type 16 (2*2+12), 2 content bytes.
9. `test_record_format_worked_example` -- Full row (42, "hello", 3.14, NULL, X'CAFE'): verify header and data match spec's worked example exactly (22 bytes total).
10. `test_record_header_size_includes_self` -- Header size varint counts itself; verify for 1-column and 10-column records.
11. `test_wal_header_magic_le` -- Little-endian machine: magic is 0x377F0682.
12. `test_wal_header_magic_be` -- Big-endian machine: magic is 0x377F0683.
13. `test_wal_header_format_version` -- Format version is always 3007000.
14. `test_wal_header_round_trip` -- Write 32-byte WAL header, read back, all fields match.
15. `test_wal_frame_header_commit` -- Commit frame: db_size > 0; non-commit frame: db_size == 0.
16. `test_wal_frame_header_salt_match` -- Frame with matching salt accepted; mismatched salt rejected.
17. `test_wal_checksum_chain_integrity` -- Multi-frame WAL: verify cumulative checksum chain.

## E2E TEST

Create a table, insert the worked-example row (42, "hello", 3.14, NULL, X'CAFE'), dump the raw record bytes from the B-tree page, and verify they match the spec's byte sequence exactly. Create a WAL-mode database, perform commits, verify WAL header and frame headers are correctly formatted, then open with C SQLite and verify data integrity.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA

- Record format matches spec exactly: header_size includes self, serial types encode type+size, content bytes are big-endian.
- Worked example row produces exactly the specified 22-byte record.
- WAL header magic correctly encodes machine endianness.
- WAL header format version is 3007000.
- WAL frame headers carry correct commit/non-commit db-size values.
- Cumulative checksum chain is correct across all frames.
- Cross-compatible: records and WAL files interoperate with C SQLite.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T06:03:35.176702581Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T21:34:57.526541775Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T21:34:57.526511118Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-lldk","depends_on_id":"bd-294","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-lldk","depends_on_id":"bd-ydbl","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1815,"issue_id":"bd-lldk","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Record Format + WAL Header + WAL Frame Header + Checksum Algorithm

### Spec Content (Lines 13899-13977, sections 11.7-11.9)

**11.7 Record Format Detail (lines 13899-13928)**

**Structure:** `[header_size: varint] [serial_types: varint...] [data: bytes...]`

The `header_size` varint includes itself (i.e., the total byte length of the header including the header_size varint).

**Serial types table:**

| Value | Type | Content Bytes |
|-------|------|---------------|
| 0 | NULL | 0 |
| 1 | 8-bit signed int | 1 |
| 2 | 16-bit big-endian signed int | 2 |
| 3 | 24-bit big-endian signed int | 3 |
| 4 | 32-bit big-endian signed int | 4 |
| 5 | 48-bit big-endian signed int | 6 |
| 6 | 64-bit big-endian signed int | 8 |
| 7 | IEEE 754 64-bit float (BE) | 8 |
| 8 | Integer constant 0 | 0 |
| 9 | Integer constant 1 | 0 |
| 10, 11 | Reserved (internal use) | - |
| N >= 12, even | BLOB of (N-12)/2 bytes | (N-12)/2 |
| N >= 13, odd | TEXT of (N-13)/2 bytes | (N-13)/2 |

**Worked example** -- Row `(42, "hello", 3.14, NULL, X'CAFE')`:
- Serial types: 1 (42 fits i8), 23 (`5*2+13`), 7 (float), 0 (NULL), 16 (`2*2+12`)
- Header: `[06, 01, 17, 07, 00, 10]` (6 bytes total including header_size varint)
- Data: `[2A]` `[68 65 6C 6C 6F]` `[40 09 1E B8 51 EB 85 1F]` `[]` `[CA FE]`
- Total: 22 bytes

**11.8 WAL Header (32 bytes) (lines 13930-13944)**

```
Offset  Size  Description
  0       4   Magic: 0x377F0682 (bigEndCksum=0, LE machine) or
              0x377F0683 (bigEndCksum=1, BE machine). See section 7.1.
  4       4   Format version: 3007000 (constant for all WAL1 databases;
              indicates WAL format from SQLite 3.7.0)
  8       4   Page size
 12       4   Checkpoint sequence number
 16       4   Salt-1
 20       4   Salt-2
 24       4   Checksum-1 (of bytes 0..23)
 28       4   Checksum-2 (of bytes 0..23)
```

**Magic numbers:**
- `0x377F0682` = little-endian checksum mode (LE machine)
- `0x377F0683` = big-endian checksum mode (BE machine)

**Format version** `3007000` is constant for all WAL1 databases.

**11.9 WAL Frame Header (24 bytes) (lines 13946-13956)**

```
Offset  Size  Description
  0       4   Page number
  4       4   For commit frames: db size in pages. Otherwise 0.
  8       4   Salt-1 (must match WAL header)
 12       4   Salt-2 (must match WAL header)
 16       4   Cumulative checksum-1
 20       4   Cumulative checksum-2
```

**11.9.1 WAL Checksum Algorithm (lines 13958-13977)**

The WAL uses a custom double-accumulator checksum (NOT CRC-32, NOT xxHash). Canonical implementation is in section 7.1 (`wal_checksum()`).

**Checksum chain:**
1. **WAL header checksum:** `wal_checksum(header_bytes[0..24], 0, 0, big_end_cksum)` -> stored at header bytes 24..32.
2. **First frame:** `wal_checksum(frame_header[0..8] ++ page_data, hdr_cksum1, hdr_cksum2, big_end_cksum)` -> stored at frame header bytes 16..24.
   - NOTE: Only the first 8 bytes of the frame header are checksummed, NOT bytes 8..16 (which contain the salt).
3. **Subsequent frames:** Use previous frame's `(cksum1, cksum2)` as seed. Each frame's checksum covers itself AND all prior frames (cumulative).

**Validation during recovery:** Walk frames sequentially. A frame is valid iff:
- Recomputed checksum matches stored values
- Salt matches WAL header salt
- First frame failing either check terminates the valid prefix of the WAL.

### Unit Tests Required

1. **test_record_encode_null**: Encode a single NULL column. Verify serial type 0, header_size=2, data is empty.
2. **test_record_encode_i8**: Encode value 42. Verify serial type 1, 1 byte of data `[0x2A]`.
3. **test_record_encode_i16**: Encode value 300. Verify serial type 2, 2 bytes big-endian data.
4. **test_record_encode_i24**: Encode value 70000. Verify serial type 3, 3 bytes big-endian data.
5. **test_record_encode_i32**: Encode value 100000. Verify serial type 4, 4 bytes big-endian data.
6. **test_record_encode_i48**: Encode value exceeding 32-bit range. Verify serial type 5, 6 bytes big-endian data.
7. **test_record_encode_i64**: Encode value exceeding 48-bit range. Verify serial type 6, 8 bytes big-endian data.
8. **test_record_encode_float**: Encode 3.14. Verify serial type 7, 8 bytes IEEE 754 big-endian.
9. **test_record_encode_constant_zero**: Encode integer 0. Verify serial type 8, 0 content bytes (optimized).
10. **test_record_encode_constant_one**: Encode integer 1. Verify serial type 9, 0 content bytes (optimized).
11. **test_record_encode_text**: Encode "hello". Verify serial type 23 (`5*2+13`), 5 bytes of UTF-8 data.
12. **test_record_encode_blob**: Encode `X'CAFE'`. Verify serial type 16 (`2*2+12`), 2 bytes of blob data.
13. **test_record_worked_example**: Encode the exact worked example from spec: `(42, "hello", 3.14, NULL, X'CAFE')`. Verify header is `[06, 01, 17, 07, 00, 10]`, total 22 bytes.
14. **test_record_decode_roundtrip**: Encode a multi-column record, decode it, verify all values match.
15. **test_record_header_size_includes_self**: Verify that the header_size varint value equals the total byte length of the header (including the header_size varint itself).
16. **test_wal_header_encode_le**: Encode a WAL header with magic `0x377F0682`, format version `3007000`, page size 4096, salts. Verify 32-byte layout.
17. **test_wal_header_encode_be**: Encode with magic `0x377F0683` (big-endian checksum). Verify magic byte difference.
18. **test_wal_header_checksum**: Compute checksum of header bytes 0..24 with seed (0, 0). Verify result matches bytes 24..32.
19. **test_wal_frame_header_encode**: Encode a frame header with page_number=5, db_size=0 (non-commit), salts matching WAL header. Verify 24-byte layout.
20. **test_wal_frame_commit_marker**: Encode a commit frame with db_size=100. Verify offset 4 contains the page count.
21. **test_wal_checksum_chain_first_frame**: Compute checksum for first frame using WAL header checksum as seed. Verify only first 8 bytes of frame header are included (NOT salt bytes 8..16).
22. **test_wal_checksum_chain_subsequent**: Compute checksums for frames 1, 2, 3 in sequence. Verify each frame's seed is the previous frame's checksum (cumulative).
23. **test_wal_frame_validation_salt_mismatch**: Create a frame with non-matching salt. Verify validation rejects it.
24. **test_wal_frame_validation_checksum_mismatch**: Corrupt a frame's checksum. Verify validation rejects it and terminates the valid prefix.

### E2E Tests

**test_e2e_record_format_compatibility**: Create a record in FrankenSQLite format, write it to a SQLite database file, open with C SQLite (if available) or verify byte-level compatibility by reading the raw cell from the page and matching the spec format exactly.

**test_e2e_wal_write_and_recover**: Write multiple transactions to WAL (multiple frames with commit markers). Simulate a crash (truncate WAL mid-frame). Recover the WAL: verify valid prefix is identified correctly, invalid frames after corruption are discarded, and recovered database state matches the last valid commit.

**test_e2e_wal_checksum_chain_integrity**: Write 100 frames to WAL, verify the entire checksum chain is valid by walking all frames sequentially and recomputing cumulative checksums.

 {"id":"bd-lq7rv","title":"WASM 1.8: End-to-end wasm32 compilation validation — cargo check --target wasm32-unknown-unknown for full workspace","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-02T04:16:57.289032188Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-07T21:56:21.453134Z","closed_at":"2026-03-07T21:56:21.453081Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-lq7rv","depends_on_id":"bd-17nh5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-lq7rv","depends_on_id":"bd-19ss5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-lq7rv","depends_on_id":"bd-1m4va","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-lq7rv","depends_on_id":"bd-34eut","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-lq7rv","depends_on_id":"bd-3rblt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-lq7rv","depends_on_id":"bd-3vwne","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1817,"issue_id":"bd-lq7rv","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# Task 1.8: End-to-End wasm32 Compilation Validation

## What
Final validation task for Phase 1. Verify the entire fsqlite workspace compiles to wasm32-unknown-unknown with appropriate feature flags.

## Validation Matrix
```bash
# Individual crate compilation
for crate in fsqlite-types fsqlite-error fsqlite-ast fsqlite-parser fsqlite-func \
             fsqlite-ext-json fsqlite-ext-misc fsqlite-vfs fsqlite-pager fsqlite-wal \
             fsqlite-mvcc fsqlite-btree fsqlite-vdbe fsqlite-planner fsqlite-core fsqlite; do
    cargo check --target wasm32-unknown-unknown -p $crate --no-default-features --features wasm
done

# Workspace-level (excluding CLI, harness, e2e, c-api)
cargo check --target wasm32-unknown-unknown -p fsqlite --no-default-features --features wasm
```

## What Must Work
1. In-memory database creation: `Connection::open(":memory:")`
2. SQL parsing: All SQL statements
3. Query planning: Full planner
4. VDBE execution: All opcodes
5. B-tree operations: Insert, delete, search
6. Built-in functions: All math, string, date functions
7. Extensions: JSON1, FTS5, R-Tree (as features)

## What Is Acceptable to Skip (Phase 1)
- File-based connections (Phase 3)
- Concurrent writers (Phase 6)
- WAL persistence (Phase 3)
- CLI (excluded from wasm)
- C API (excluded from wasm)
- Test harness (excluded from wasm)
- E2E tests (excluded from wasm)

## Binary Size Baseline
Capture initial wasm binary size for tracking:
```bash
cargo build --target wasm32-unknown-unknown -p fsqlite --release --no-default-features --features wasm
wasm-opt -Os target/wasm32-unknown-unknown/release/fsqlite.wasm -o fsqlite-opt.wasm
ls -la fsqlite-opt.wasm  # Record baseline size
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- All targeted crates compile to wasm32 with zero errors
- Binary size baseline recorded and documented
- No clippy warnings (pedantic+nursery) on wasm32 target
- Native compilation and tests completely unaffected
